text: The intrusion began with a spearphishing email carrying a weaponized Microsoft Word attachment. When the victim opened the document and enabled content, an embedded macro launched a PowerShell one-liner that downloaded the second stage. The malware established persistence by writing a value under the HKCU Run registry key so it relaunched at each logon. Operators later dumped credentials from LSASS process memory to harvest domain hashes, then exfiltrated the collected data back to the actor's command-and-control server over the existing C2 channel.

Access was obtained after a user clicked a malicious link in a phishing email and entered credentials on a lookalike portal. Using those valid accounts, the actor logged in over RDP to a jump host. They created a scheduled task to run the payload every hour, and disabled the endpoint's antivirus and tamper protection before deployment. In the final stage the ransomware encrypted files across mapped drives and deleted volume shadow copies to prevent recovery.

Post-compromise, the operators relied heavily on living-off-the-land techniques. They used Windows Management Instrumentation to execute commands remotely across hosts. Additional tooling was pulled onto victims by downloading binaries from an attacker-controlled URL. The payloads were packed and heavily obfuscated to hinder analysis. Command-and-control traffic was tunneled over standard HTTPS web traffic to blend in with normal activity.

The actor conducted a password brute-force campaign against an internet-facing service until a valid credential was found. They then moved laterally by connecting to administrative SMB shares (C$/ADMIN$) on neighboring servers. On each host, execution was achieved by creating and starting a Windows service pointing at the implant. Throughout, the operators ran commands to enumerate other reachable systems on the internal network.

Initial access was gained by exploiting a known vulnerability in an internet-facing web application. The adversary dropped a web shell into the server's web root to maintain access. Through the web shell they executed reconnaissance commands via the Windows command shell (cmd.exe). Sensitive documents were then compressed and uploaded to an attacker-controlled cloud storage bucket for exfiltration.

This macOS-focused campaign started with a spearphishing attachment disguised as an invoice. On open, the malware invoked AppleScript via osascript to run its logic. It installed a Launch Agent plist in the user's Library so it would persist across reboots. The implant then read stored credentials from the login keychain and periodically captured the user's screen, staging the images for later collection.

Investigators found that AWS access keys had been committed to a public GitHub repository. The actor used these valid cloud credentials to authenticate to the victim's AWS account. They enumerated the available cloud services and permissions, then created an additional set of IAM access keys to retain access. Finally, they downloaded sensitive objects from several S3 storage buckets.

The attacker brute-forced SSH credentials on an internet-exposed Linux host. After logging in they downloaded a payload with wget from a remote server. Persistence was achieved by adding a cron job. They ran a Unix shell script that deployed a cryptocurrency miner, consuming the machine's CPU, and cleared the bash command history and the system logs to cover their tracks.

The group gained access through a compromised VPN account that was not protected by multi-factor authentication. They used the ESXi shell to run commands, terminated the running virtual machine processes to unlock the files, and then encrypted the VMDK datastores. Backups reachable on the network were deleted to prevent recovery.

An employee received a phishing link leading to a fake Microsoft 365 login page and entered their credentials. The attacker signed in to the cloud account and created an inbox rule to auto-forward and hide financial emails. They repeatedly triggered MFA push notifications until the user approved one. Using the compromised mailbox, they collected internal emails and sent fraudulent wire-transfer requests to the finance team.

Distributed via a cracked application download, the macOS stealer prompted the user for their system password using a fake dialog box. It used osascript to display the prompt and run its AppleScript logic. The malware then extracted passwords from the login keychain, stole saved passwords and cookies from web browsers, and collected files from the local system. The archive of stolen data was uploaded to the attacker's server over HTTP.

The threat actor compromised a managed service provider and abused the existing trusted connection to reach downstream customer networks. They used the MSP's remote monitoring and management software to push an installer to endpoints. DLL side-loading was used to run the malicious payload under a legitimate signed application. The implant injected code into a running process and communicated with its command-and-control server over DNS.

After exploiting a vulnerability in a public-facing application, the actor uploaded a web shell for persistence. They dumped credentials from LSASS memory, then performed Kerberoasting to obtain service-account hashes. Using the cracked credentials they moved laterally with Windows Remote Management. They dumped the NTDS.dit domain database from a domain controller and modified a Group Policy Object to deploy their payload across the domain.

A malicious ISO attachment contained a shortcut file that, when clicked by the user, executed rundll32 to load a Cobalt Strike beacon. The loader used mshta to fetch an additional script. The actor bypassed User Account Control to elevate privileges and disabled Windows Defender through registry modifications. Beacon traffic blended in by using HTTPS web protocols.

A departing employee used their valid access to collect proprietary documents from an internal network file share. They archived the files into a password-protected RAR archive and copied it to a USB drive. They also uploaded several documents to a personal cloud storage account.

The infection was fileless: a phishing document ran a PowerShell command that loaded the payload directly into memory. Persistence was established through a WMI event subscription that re-launched the implant on system startup. The malware queried the registry, enumerated running processes, and identified installed security products. It reflectively loaded a .NET assembly to avoid writing anything to disk.

The banking trojan arrived as a malicious email attachment that the victim opened. Once running, it logged the victim's keystrokes and monitored the clipboard to steal copied cryptocurrency wallet addresses. It harvested saved credentials from web browsers and injected into the browser process to intercept online banking sessions. Stolen data was sent to the command-and-control server over an encrypted channel.

Initial access was through an exposed RDP service reachable from the internet using a compromised local administrator account. The actor ran built-in Windows commands to discover domain accounts, network shares, and reachable hosts. They deployed a commercial remote access tool and used PsExec to execute the ransomware across the domain by creating a service on each host. Before encryption they stopped backup and database services.

Victims were compromised via a drive-by download after visiting a legitimate but compromised website. The exploit triggered a browser vulnerability to execute code on the endpoint. A malicious browser extension was installed to maintain access and capture data entered into web forms. The payload established a SOCKS proxy so the actor could tunnel traffic into the internal network.

The wiper was delivered through a compromised software update mechanism. On execution it corrupted the master boot record and overwrote files with random data, rendering systems unbootable. It also cleared the Windows event logs and deleted volume shadow copies. The malware spread to other hosts by copying itself to accessible administrative shares.
